peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.
High openness and internal political efficacy drive support for transparency, but low trust in institutions and moderate libertarian anti-censorship views temper enthusiasm to a mild level.

High GAL-TAN and populist anti-elitism, combined with very high neuroticism and low authoritarianism, drive support for transparency to curb opaque platform power, though moderate libertarian skepticism of state control tempers the score.

High conscientiousness and very low openness to experience combine with strong authoritarian law-and-order preferences to produce moderate support for mandated transparency as a rule-based accountability measure, though low libertarian anti-censorship orientation tempers enthusiasm.

High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High openness and agreeableness, combined with strong fairness/cheating concerns (low) and high care/harm, lead to moderate support for transparency as a protective measure, but libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) tempers it.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and reciprocity values combined with high dutifulness produce clear support for disclosure, though low trust in institutions prevents maximal endorsement.

Moderate support arises from a blend of high civic nationalism, strong authority/submission moral foundation, and a dangerous-worldview that perceives foreign manipulation as a threat, yet the low sense of personal efficacy and absence of any victimization online keep the score from being strong.

Strong loyalty/ingroup morality and a dangerous worldview make this person want to expose inauthentic actors, yet low digital literacy and intermittent access reduce personal urgency to a clear but not strong support.

Strong pluralism and civil liberties orientation, plus high internal political efficacy, support disclosure to protect democratic discourse, but low trust in institutions and a fearful-avoidant attachment style moderate enthusiasm for enforcement.

Very high neuroticism and a dangerous-worldview score near zero, paired with high dutifulness and strong support for coercive social control, drive clear support for disclosure rules to reduce perceived online threats.

Strong moral foundations for fairness/reciprocity and authority/subversion, plus high trust in police and government, drive clear support for disclosure to preserve social order, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.

High care/harm (87.29) and moderate social dominance orientation (73.96) create a cautious endorsement of disclosure to protect vulnerable users, though low libertarian privacy (20.54) reduces full enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and achiever orientation align with educational solutions, while low openness limits enthusiasm for novel curricula, yielding strong but not maximal support.

Strong support is driven by the person's very high conscientiousness, high achievement striving, strong Philanthropist HEXAD profile, and the belief that education aligns with respect for tradition and a duty to protect the national community from disinformation.
Very high conscientiousness and a philanthropic HEXAD profile align with educational solutions, while high openness to experience makes this an appealing systemic fix, yielding strong support.

Very high extraversion and HEXACO openness, combined with a philanthropist user type and high people-centrism, strongly favor educational empowerment, while basic digital literacy and low conscientiousness prevent maximal endorsement.

High conscientiousness and very high openness to experience (HEXACO) alongside strong GAL values and a prosocial philanthropic orientation produce firm support for educational interventions as a non-coercive solution.

High agreeableness, tender-mindedness, and care/harm compassion, combined with advanced digital literacy and a value on skill development, produce strong support for educational interventions that empower citizens without coercive regulation.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.
High openness (69.03) and strong altruism (92.88) drive strong support for educational interventions, while low authoritarian submission (19.56) allows endorsement without fear of indoctrination.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low libertarianism and high authoritarian submission are offset by low trust in government and a dangerous worldview, producing mild opposition to a new agency.

Clear opposition emerges because, despite high trust in government, the person's extreme authoritarian submission and powerful collective civic nationalism reject any agency that could be perceived as undermining national unity or usurping local authority, while high political trust ironically makes them see such agencies as unnecessary.

Despite a dangerous worldview, low trust in government and moderate authoritarian submission are offset by high anti-elitism and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style that resists institutional oversight, producing mild opposition.

Strong anti-authoritarianism and low deference to authority, paired with high populist anti-elitism and a disruptive user type, generate opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially coercive.

Low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and moderate trust in government are offset by very low openness to experience and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style, yielding mild opposition to a centralized agency perceived as potentially intrusive.

Despite high trust in government and authoritarian submission, very low libertarian civil liberties support and high collective narcissism (low) actually reduce fear of foreign influence, while moderate openness and a history of protest participation create mild opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially overreaching.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and judiciary (6.29) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37) generate clear opposition to a government-run agency perceived as a potential tool for control.

Low libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) and high system justification (85.5) create mild opposition to a centralized agency, as it conflicts with distrust of state control (10.55) and preference for organic solutions.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with high fairness concerns, drive clear opposition to restricting foreign media as unfair and illiberal.

Strong support flows from very high ethnic and civic nationalism scores, an extremely high dangerous-worldview (93.47), and a restrictive outgroup-policy preference (80.54), all of which frame foreign media as a vector for harmful influence that must be contained by a trusted state.
Low ethnic nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with very high openness and low authoritarian aggression, lead to clear opposition against restricting foreign media.

Extremely low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with very high cultural openness and low authoritarianism, produce strong opposition to restricting foreign media as xenophobic and illiberal.
Low ethnic nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with high GAL pluralism support and a second-generation migration background, drive clear opposition to restricting foreign media.

Extremely high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), ethnic nationalism (77%), and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (88.8%) combine with high authority/subversion and sanctity/purity to produce strong support for restricting foreign media to protect national unity and cultural integrity.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (92.61), national superiority beliefs (79.72), and a competitive jungle worldview (87.64) combined with very low openness to cultural diversity (socio-cultural liberal-conservative mean 35.3) drive strong support for restricting foreign media influence.
High cosmopolitan orientation (26.62) and low ethnic nationalism (36.61) produce clear opposition to restricting foreign media, seeing it as protectionist and counter to global information flow.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarian submission and conventionalism, plus low libertarianism, produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation, favoring order over unregulated speech.

Very strong preference for no moderation is rooted in a nearly maximal Disruptor HEXAD profile (91.05), extraordinarily high Eysenck psychoticism (coldness 96.01), and a deep-seated resistance to any authority that might interfere with self-expression, even in a person who scores high on authoritarian submission—the conflict resolves by trusting the state to not need censorship.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and low authoritarian aggression push toward preferring less moderation, yet a dangerous worldview and high conscientiousness create ambivalence, resulting in mild support.

Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high disruptor traits favor minimal moderation, but very high neuroticism and a dangerous-worldview sensitivity create a countervailing desire for protection from harmful content.

Very high authoritarianism and low libertarian civil liberties support, combined with high conscientiousness and a strong law-and-order preference, produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation.

Very high authoritarian submission (92%), conventionalism (81%), and punitive attitudes (17.5% but in context of high RWA) combine with low openness and high conscientiousness to produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation, favoring strong platform rules to maintain social order.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (57.37), strong free spirit user type (67.34), and very low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with distrust of institutions produce clear support for minimal moderation, valuing individual autonomy over platform control.

Low libertarian anti-censorship (10.41) and high agreeableness (77.08) lead to mild opposition to complete freedom from moderation, as it could enable harm, though high openness values (70.06) create some ambivalence.

Near-neutrality reflects a clash between strong trust in government and high civic nationalism (favoring state-backed tools) versus the person's very low digital deprivation, high digital confidence, and a free-spirit autonomy that dislikes being told which tools to trust.
High openness and philanthropic values favor open-source tools, but low trust in government and moderate economic conservatism about public spending produce near-neutral leaning mild support.
Moderate economic leftism and high trust in science and experts support public funding for trust tools, while low trust in government and a preference for autonomy temper the score.
High GAL participatory democracy preference and moderate trust in government support funding, but low openness to experience and a skeptical view of state control keep support mild.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness and reciprocity values, plus high fact-checking behavior, produce clear support for independent fact-checking, though low trust in experts prevents a higher score.

Clear opposition arises from an extremely high traditional-authoritarian-nationalist cluster (mean 90.98) paired with a very low libertarian civil-liberties orientation (5.34), a strong dual-process competitive-jungle worldview, and high Eysenckian coldness—all rejecting the premise that an independent, often liberal, fact-checking body is trustworthy or needed.

Strong loyalty/ingroup morality and a dangerous worldview make fact-checking appealing to protect the ingroup, while high trust in science and experts reinforces clear support for independent verification.

High trust in science and experts, combined with high fact-checking behavior and a philanthropist user type, strongly support independent fact-checking, though low agreeableness and moderate populist anti-elitism prevent uncritical endorsement.

High moral foundation of care/harm and moderate trust in science, combined with high dutifulness and a prosocial orientation, produce moderate support for independent fact-checking as a non-governmental solution.

High agreeableness, trust in institutions, and fairness/reciprocity moral foundation drive moderate support for independent fact-checking as a balanced approach, though low openness and high authoritarianism temper full endorsement of decentralized oversight.

Moderate openness (57.59) and high care/harm foundation (93.03) provide some support for fact-checking, but very low trust in science (2.57) and experts, combined with a competitive worldview (87.64), keep support mild and conditional on independence from government.

High care/harm (87.29) and strong openness to ideas (58.63) drive clear support for independent fact-checking as a corrective, while low authoritarian submission (19.56) ensures it remains non-coercive.
